---
title: "Problem Set 8"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(cache = TRUE, fig.align = "center", include = FALSE) # set include = TRUE to see solutions in knitted RMarkdown
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
```


# Logistic Regression

We will now continue modeling data with logistic regression using * [nfl_wp_train.csv](data/nfl_wp_train.csv) to analyze win percentages as a function of multiple variables. The dataset includes `game_id`, `season`, `label_win`, which is a binary (0 or 1) indicator on whether the possessing team won the game, `score_differential`, which is the current score differential between the team in possession and the team on defense, `posteam_spread`, which is the +- spread for the possessing team prior to the game start, `yardline_100`, `down`, and `ydstogo`. 

1. First, load in the data. Previously, two different datasets have been provided for test and train. We can do this ourselves by running the following code which may also be helpful for your projects. For the first half of the problem set, use the training data.
```{r split data, include = TRUE}
nfl_wp = read.csv('data/nfl_wp_train.csv')

set.seed(42)

#get unique games   
unique_game_ids <- unique(nfl_wp$game_id)
# randomly sample 80% of the game ids for training, rounding down to nearest integer
train_game_ids <- sample(unique_game_ids, size = floor(0.8 * length(unique_game_ids))) 

#filter for train and test 
nfl_train <- nfl_wp %>% filter(game_id %in% train_game_ids) 
nfl_test <- nfl_wp %>% filter(!(game_id %in% train_game_ids))
```


2. Next, do some exploratory data analysis (EDA) to understand the data. First, plot a histogram of `score_differential` to show the distribution of the variable. Then, make a separate data table grouped by game and select the first instance of `posteam_spread` so that we have only one per game. Plot the distribution of this variable.

```{r}

nfl_wp_grouped <- nfl_train %>%
  group_by(game_id) %>%
  reframe(
    spread = first(posteam_spread)
  ) 

ggplot(nfl_wp_grouped, aes(x = spread)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 2, fill = "lightblue4") +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Team Spread",
       x = "Spread",
       y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()

ggplot(nfl_train, aes(x = score_differential)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 1, fill = "lightcoral") +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Score Differential",
       x = "Spread",
       y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()
```

3. Then fit a logistic model called `diff_model` using `glm` to predict `label_win` based on `score_differential`. Call the model after you fit it to see the coefficients and then add its predictions to the dataset.
```{r model and predictions.}

diff_model = glm(label_win ~ score_differential, data = nfl_wp)
diff_model 
nfl_wp <- nfl_wp %>% 
  mutate(diff_predicted_win = predict(diff_model, type = "response"))
nfl_wp
```

4. Repeat step 3 but predicting with `posteam_spread` instead. 
```{r}
spread_model = glm(label_win ~ posteam_spread, data = nfl_wp)
spread_model 
nfl_wp <- nfl_wp %>% 
  mutate(spread_predicted_win = predict(spread_model, type = "response"))
nfl_wp
```

5. Now, plot the predicted probabilities of winning based on the two models you created in steps 3 and 4. Put the predictor variable on the x-axis and the predicted win probability on the y axis. Color the points by the label_win variable using `as.factor()`. Which variable seems to predict better? 
```{r}
ggplot(nfl_wp, aes(x = score_differential, y = diff_predicted_win, color = as.factor(label_win))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(title = "Predicted Win Probability vs Score Differential",
       x = "Score Differential",
       y = "Predicted Win Probability",
       color = "Actual Win") +
  theme_minimal()

ggplot(nfl_wp, aes(x = posteam_spread, y = spread_predicted_win, color = as.factor(label_win))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(title = "Predicted Win Probability vs Spread",
       x = "Spread",
       y = "Predicted Win Probability",
       color = "Actual Win") +
  theme_minimal()
```

6. Finally, let's make a model to account for both variables. Name your model `spread_diff_model` and follow the previous steps to obtain predictions and plot the data as a function of score differential (since this variable is more continuous than spread as we saw with our histogram).
```{r}
spread_diff_model = glm(label_win ~ score_differential + posteam_spread, data = nfl_wp)
nfl_wp <- nfl_wp %>% 
  mutate(spread_diff_predicted_win = predict(spread_diff_model, type = "response"))
ggplot(nfl_wp, aes(x = score_differential, y = spread_diff_predicted_win, color = as.factor(label_win))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(title = "Predicted Win Probability vs Score Differential (with Spread)",
       x = "Score Differential",
       y = "Predicted Win Probability",
       color = "Actual Win") +
  theme_minimal()
```

7. Let's test all of our models. Calculate the RMSE of the three different model predictions. Which model was the best? 


*Challenge: Can you make an even better model with the other variables in the dataset or with numerical transformations to variables?*
